title: Jesus’ love for his father part 4
scripture_text: ‘’After Jesus said this, he looked toward heaven and prayed: “Father, the hour has come. Glorify your Son, that your Son may glorify you. For you granted him authority over all people that he might give eternal life to all those you have given him. Now this is eternal life: that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom you have sent. I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do. And now, Father, glorify me in your presence with the glory I had with you before the world began.’’
devotional:
Jesus did not use his authority to protect himself from vulnerability.

Let me try and explain what ‘authority’ means. Imagine I went into your home, I go and put the kettle on, I turn on the television, I sit down with a coffee, put my feet on the table, and as I watch TV I open the post. Then I go online and check your bank balance. The problem is, I don’t have the authority to do any of those things. Jesus was given authority or freedom to exercise his rule over all people, but he chose not to.

Notice that Jesus does not merely say that the Father gave Him authority only over His disciples and those who would later believe. Jesus says that the Father gave Him authority “over all humanity.” Jesus had full authority over Judas, over the high priests, and those Roman officials who were instrumental in His death. Jesus had complete authority over the hostile mob, who cried out, “Crucify! Crucify!

Do you remember when they came to arrest Him? John tells us 19;2 ‘Now Judas, who betrayed him, knew the place, because Jesus had often met there with his disciples. 3.So Judas came to the garden, guiding a detachment of soldiers and some officials from the chief priests and the Pharisees. They were carrying torches, lanterns and weapons. 4. Jesus, knowing all that was going to happen to him, went out and asked them, “Who is it you want?” 5. “Jesus of Nazareth,” they replied. “I am he,” Jesus said. (And Judas the traitor was standing there with them.) When Jesus said, “I am he,” they drew back and fell to the ground.’

Jesus had authority to defend himself, he could have destroyed his enemies, but instead he chose the way of vulnerability, weakness and sacrifice. Father you have given me authority, but I am not going to use my authority to protect myself, but I am going to use it to bring eternal life to those you have given me!

Jesus showed his love for his Father by embracing vulnerability and refusing to take control. He would only use his authority for the life giving benefit for others.

As children of God, we need to love our Father God by relinquishing our need to be in control, and our need to protect ourselves, so we will have the authority or freedom to minister to others in ways that are effective.

5. As a Son, Jesus recognised the importance of accountability.

Look at what Jesus says, ‘I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do.’ Here Jesus is reporting back to his Father. Try and pick up the heart behind these words. ‘I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do.’ For Jesus, genuine prayer contained an expression of accountability.

I find this very challenging, we express our love for the father by being conscientious about our accountability. We will never be able to pray these words that Jesus prayed, but we can make ourselves more accountable, we can critique our faithfulness. From time to time we need to come before God and by his grace evaluate where we are, where we have come from and where we are going. Why? That is what a child of God does, that is the kind of spiritual discipline that helps us develop our relationship with the father.

6. As a son Jesus recognised that fellowship with the father was the best thing in his life. In verse 5 Jesus says these words, ‘And now, Father, glorify me in your presence with the glory I had with you before the world began.’

There is a lot of mystery in this verse, a lot that is outside our understanding. However, it is also one of the most incredible verses in the bible that can teach us about the essence of son-ship. Jesus was about to go to the cross, he was going to take away the sin of the world, he was going to defeat death and Satan. He was going to ascend on high, be given all authority in heaven and earth, and be crowned king of kings. But what brought him the greatest joy, the greatest glory can be summed up it two words, ‘with you.’ Those two simple words verbalised Jesus’ deepest desire, the longing of his heart.

What he would achieve through his death and resurrection would change heaven and earth, but they would not replace the ‘with you,’ of Jesus heart.

Like Jesus we need a ‘with you,’ desire, a ‘with you,’ motivation, a realisation that ‘with you,’ sums up what life is all about. Jesus died so we could have heart of a child of God, a heart that could continually say Father, the real glory of life is only found in your presence, with you.
prayer: Father thank you that two words sum the goal and purpose of all of life ‘with you.’
html:
Jesus’ love for his father part 4

Study Text: John 17 v 1-5
‘’After Jesus said this, he looked toward heaven and prayed: “Father, the hour has come. Glorify your Son, that your Son may glorify you. For you granted him authority over all people that he might give eternal life to all those you have given him. Now this is eternal life: that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom you have sent. I have brought you glory on earth by finishing the work you gave me to do. And now, Father, glorify me in your presence with the glory I had with you before the world began.’’

Jesus did not use his authority to protect himself from vulnerability.

Let me try and explain what ‘authority’ means. Imagine I went into your home, I go and put the kettle on, I turn on the television, I sit down with a coffee, put my feet on the table, and as I watch TV I open the post. Then I go online and check your bank balance. The problem is, I don’t have the authority to do any of those things. Notice that Jesus does not merely say that the Father gave Him authority only over His disciples and those who would later believe. John tells us 19;2 ‘Now Judas, who betrayed him, knew the place, because Jesus had often met there with his disciples. 3.So Judas came to the garden, guiding a detachment of soldiers and some officials from the chief priests and the Pharisees. They were carrying torches, lanterns and weapons. 4. Jesus, knowing all that was going to happen to him, went out and asked them, “Who is it you want?” 5. “Jesus of Nazareth,” they replied. “I am he,” Jesus said. (And Judas the traitor was standing there with them.) When Jesus said, “I am he,” they drew back and fell to the ground.’

Father you have given me authority, but I am not going to use my authority to protect myself, but I am going to use it to bring eternal life to those you have given me!

As children of God, we need to love our Father God by relinquishing our need to be in control, and our need to protect ourselves, so we will have the authority or freedom to minister to others in ways that are effective.

6. As a son Jesus recognised that fellowship with the father was the best thing in his life. In verse 5 Jesus says these words, ‘And now, Father, glorify me in your presence with the glory I had with you before the world began.’

There is a lot of mystery in this verse, a lot that is outside our understanding. However, it is also one of the most incredible verses in the bible that can teach us about the essence of son-ship. Jesus was about to go to the cross, he was going to take away the sin of the world, he was going to defeat death and Satan. He was going to ascend on high, be given all authority in heaven and earth, and be crowned king of kings. But what brought him the greatest joy, the greatest glory can be summed up it two words, ‘with you.’ Those two simple words verbalised Jesus’ deepest desire, the longing of his heart.

What he would achieve through his death and resurrection would change heaven and earth, but they would not replace the ‘with you,’ of Jesus heart.

Like Jesus we need a ‘with you,’ desire, a ‘with you,’ motivation, a realisation that ‘with you,’ sums up what life is all about. Jesus died so we could have heart of a child of God, a heart that could continually say Father, the real glory of life is only found in your presence, with you.

Prayer: Father thank you that two words sum the goal and purpose of all of life ‘with you.’
Bible in a year reading plan: - 2 Kings 17-19
